data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setForNextEpoch signature changed — the last parameter is uint64 _autoEvictThresholdPct (0-100), not uint256 _autoEvictThreshold. The old uint256 field is preserved in storage as __deprecated_autoEvictThreshold (line 71) but is never read by downstream logic.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Caller must be GOVERNANCE; threshold must satisfy 0-100 range check in _validateConfig.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Parameter type changed from uint256 to uint64, semantics changed from raw proposal count to percentage, and the value is now range-checked.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _validateConfig now accepts _autoEvictThresholdPct as a 6th parameter. Line 301-303 explicitly enforces `if (_autoEvictThresholdPct > 100) revert InvalidAutoEvictThresholdPct(_autoEvictThresholdPct, 100)`. Passing type(uint256).max is impossible (uint64) and any value > 100 reverts.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: _autoEvictThresholdPct must be <= 100 at both initialize() and setForNextEpoch() paths.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Upper-bound validation was explicitly added to _validateConfig.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: PendingConfig.autoEvictThreshold is removed (replaced by __deprecated_autoEvictThreshold which is hardcoded to 0 at line 217). autoEvictThresholdPct (uint64, validated <=100) is stored at line 218.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Field is different type, different semantics, and cannot hold an unbounded value.

data_flow_trace.original: applyPendingConfig() writes threshold=type(uint256).max to live storage
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: applyPendingConfig still applies pending config at epoch boundary, but it copies autoEvictThresholdPct (bounded 0-100), not an unbounded uint256. Line 248: `autoEvictThresholdPct = _pendingConfig.autoEvictThresholdPct`.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Caller must be RECONFIGURATION; hasPendingConfig must be true.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Value propagated is range-checked (<=100), not unbounded.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Function reads `uint64 thresholdPct = IValidatorConfig(...).autoEvictThresholdPct()`. It is a uint64 percentage bounded to [0,100]; it cannot be type(uint256).max.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Only runs if allowValidatorSetChange=true (line 617) AND closingEpoch > 1 (line 624) AND autoEvictEnabled=true (line 665).
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Read value is a percentage, not a raw count.

data_flow_trace.original: `perfs[i].successfulProposals <= type(uint256).max` evaluates true for every validator
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: New logic: `successPct = successfulProposals * 100 / total; shouldEvict = successPct < thresholdPct`. With thresholdPct capped at 100, shouldEvict can only be true for genuinely underperforming validators. A validator with >= thresholdPct success rate is never evicted, even at the maximum valid thresholdPct=100 (where a 100% success rate still survives).
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Special cases: total==0 OR successfulProposals==0 force eviction — but these indicate a genuinely dead validator, independent of threshold manipulation.

data_flow_trace.original: Liveness guard breaks only when remainingActive <= 1; N-1 validators marked PENDING_INACTIVE
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Liveness guard at line 713 still permits shrinking to 1 validator. However, this is only reachable if shouldEvict is true for all validators — which now requires all validators to genuinely underperform, not governance misconfiguration.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Guard unchanged, but upstream attack vector (forcing shouldEvict=true via threshold manipulation) is eliminated.

reasoning_chain: The exact attack described — setting autoEvictThreshold to type(uint256).max so that every validator's successfulProposals <= threshold — is fully mitigated. The field has been refactored from an unbounded uint256 raw count to a uint64 percentage (autoEvictThresholdPct) validated to [0,100] in _validateConfig (ValidatorConfig.sol:301-303). The eviction comparison was rewritten from `successfulProposals <= threshold` to `successPct < thresholdPct` where successPct is capped at 100 (ValidatorManagement.sol:705-708). At the maximum legal thresholdPct=100, only validators with <100% success rate are eviction candidates, not all validators. Moreover, there is now an allowValidatorSetChange freeze guard (line 617) and an epoch>1 guard (line 624). The old uint256 field persists only as __deprecated_autoEvictThreshold for storage layout and is never read.

key_evidence.why_matters: Explicit upper bound validation that the original issue claimed was missing.
The function now passes the threshold into _validateConfig (which the original issue flagged as missing) and its type is uint64, not uint256.
Shows the old unbounded field was deliberately deprecated and replaced with a bounded percentage.

key_evidence.excerpt: uint256 successPct = (uint256(perfs[i].successfulProposals) * 100) / total; if (successPct < thresholdPct) { shouldEvict = true; }
key_evidence.why_matters: Comparison rewritten as percentage-based; forcing `shouldEvict=true` for all validators via threshold=MAX is no longer possible because successPct is bounded to [0,100] and thresholdPct is capped at 100.
Additional defense-in-depth: when the validator-set-change freeze is active, eviction is skipped entirely.

counterargument: One could argue that governance (if compromised) can still set autoEvictThresholdPct=100, which evicts every validator whose success rate is strictly less than 100%. In a realistic network, almost no validator has a perfect 100% success rate, so setting thresholdPct=100 could still cause mass eviction. However: (a) the liveness guard at line 713 always preserves at least 1 active validator, (b) the Phase 1 underbonded guard also runs and shares the same counter, (c) this now requires validators to have actually missed proposals (i.e., it is no longer a pure configuration exploit — it piggybacks on real performance data). More importantly, the SPECIFIC attack described in the issue — `successfulProposals <= type(uint256).max` evaluating true for every validator regardless of performance — no longer applies because the comparison is percentage-based and the threshold cannot be set to type(uint256).max. The residual risk of `thresholdPct=100` is a different, weaker issue and out of scope for this verdict.
